steps.title: Focus on predator learning
steps.content: Mora and colleagues claim that clicks and chirps in caterpillars with chemical defenses mainly warn or educate predators. The support should therefore connect those short-duration sounds to learning from consequences, not merely to being loud, easy to hear, or capable of startling predators during an attack.

steps.title: Connect short sounds to warning
steps.content: Choice B concerns volume, which could make a sound noticeable but does not show that predators are being educated. Choice C concerns audibility to predators, not learning. Choice D says predators stop attacks in response to sounds of any duration, which fits startling more than warning. The useful finding is that short sounds such as clicks and chirps are effective in training animals to associate behavior with a consequence. The answer is A.
